votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the result equals the product of the absolute values of a and b modulo 10.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, computing the product of the last digits of the absolute values of a and b and comparing it to the result.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the result equals the product of the last digits of the absolute values of a and b.

The Python code directly implements the Coq specification: it checks whether result equals (abs(a) mod 10) * (abs(b) mod 10). Python's abs() and % operator on integers behave the same as Coq's Z.abs and Z.modulo for the relevant cases (taking last digit of absolute value), producing identical concrete results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute result == (|a| mod 10) * (|b| mod 10). Python's abs() matches Z.abs, Python's % operator on non-negative integers matches Z.modulo, and the multiplication and equality check are equivalent. The Coq Prop is translated to a boolean predicate that returns true when the proposition would hold.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both compute result == (|a| mod 10) * (|b| mod 10). Python's abs() matches Z.abs, Python's % operator on non-negative integers (which abs() guarantees) matches Z.modulo, and multiplication is the same. The Python function returns a boolean indicating whether the equality holds, which is the natural way to check the Coq Prop on concrete inputs.
Both compute (|a| mod 10) * (|b| mod 10) and compare to result; Python ints and modulo behavior match Coq Z for positive modulus.
Both compute (abs(a) mod 10) * (abs(b) mod 10) and compare to result; Python ints and % behavior match Coq Z and mod 10 semantics.
Both compute (abs(a) % 10) * (abs(b) % 10) and check equality with result. Since abs makes inputs nonnegative, modulo behavior matches in Coq and Python, and Python integers are unbounded.
